content.text: Automated Architecture Tests for Claude Systems

Architectural fitness functions are automated tests that verify a system continues to conform to its intended architecture as it evolves. For Claude integrations, architecture erosion takes specific forms: prompt strings drifting into application code, direct SDK usage spreading beyond the designated integration layer, observability gaps accumulating across new endpoints. Fitness functions catch these before they become load-bearing technical debt.

attrs.alt: Five fitness function categories for Claude systems: Dependency, Observability, Security, Resilience, and Prompt Governance

content.text: What Makes a Good Fitness Function
A fitness function is only useful if it runs automatically on every relevant change and produces a clear pass/fail result. The criteria for a well-designed fitness function:
Automated and CI-enforced:
runs in CI without manual invocation. A fitness function that requires a human to remember to run it is not a fitness function — it is a checklist.
Fast enough to not be bypassed:
static analysis fitness functions run in seconds and can run on every commit. Fitness functions that make API calls should run only on relevant file changes, not on every commit.
Precise failure output:
the failure message tells the developer exactly which file and line violated the architecture constraint, not just that a violation exists.
Difficult to accidentally satisfy:
a fitness function that passes because a file was accidentally excluded from the scan is worse than no fitness function — it creates false confidence.
Category 1 — Dependency Fitness Functions
Enforce the integration layer boundary

One of the most common architecture erosions in Claude applications is the gradual spread of direct Anthropic SDK imports across the codebase. When every module imports from anthropic directly, there is no central place to enforce configuration standards (max_retries, timeout, API key source) and no single point to intercept for testing. A dependency fitness function enforces that only designated modules may import the SDK:
Enforce singleton client

Multiple Anthropic client instances in the same process each maintain their own connection pool and retry state. This wastes file descriptors and makes rate limit tracking inconsistent. A fitness function verifies that the client is constructed only once:
Category 2 — Observability Fitness Functions
Every API call module must import structlog
If a module calls the Claude API but does not import structlog, it is making unlogged calls. A fitness function detects modules that import anthropic without also importing structlog:
Category 3 — Security Fitness Functions
Semgrep as a fitness function

Semgrep rules run as part of CI static analysis and function as architectural fitness functions when they detect patterns that violate the security architecture. Unlike ad-hoc linting, Semgrep rules are versioned alongside the codebase and enforced on every PR:

# .semgrep/fitness-functions.yml
rules:
  - id: ff-no-inline-prompt-string
    patterns:
      - pattern: |
          $CLIENT.messages.create(..., system="...", ...)
      - pattern-not: |
          $CLIENT.messages.create(..., system=$VAR, ...)
    message: >
      System prompt is an inline string literal. Prompts must be loaded from
      the template registry, not hardcoded. Use registry.get('template-id').system_prompt.
    severity: ERROR
    languages: [python]

  - id: ff-no-print-of-messages
    patterns:
      - pattern-either:
          - pattern: print($RESP.content)
          - pattern: print($MSG["content"])
          - pattern: logger.debug(..., messages=$MSGS, ...)
    message: >
      Model messages or content printed/logged at debug level.
      Use structlog with sanitised fields; never log raw message content.
    severity: ERROR
    languages: [python]

  - id: ff-response-content-access-without-type-check
    patterns:
      - pattern: $RESP.content[$IDX].text
      - pattern-not: |
          if $RESP.content[$IDX].type == "text":
              $RESP.content[$IDX].text
    message: >
      Content block .text accessed without checking .type == 'text'.
      ToolUseBlock does not have a .text attribute; this will raise AttributeError
      in tool-use responses.
    severity: WARNING
    languages: [python]
Category 4 — Resilience Fitness Functions
Verify circuit breaker is wired

A circuit breaker that exists in the code but is not connected to the API call path provides no protection. A fitness function verifies that the circuit breaker call wraps the actual API call in every integration module:
Category 5 — Prompt Governance Fitness Functions
No inline system prompt strings in application code

System prompts that live in application code cannot be reviewed through the prompt review pipeline, cannot have changelogs, and cannot be rolled back independently of the code. A fitness function detects inline prompt strings:
Running Fitness Functions in CI

# .github/workflows/fitness-functions.yml
name: Architecture Fitness Functions

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  fitness-functions:
    name: Architecture Fitness
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - run: pip install pytest semgrep

      - name: Dependency fitness functions
        run: pytest tests/architecture/test_dependency_fitness.py -v

      - name: Observability fitness functions
        run: pytest tests/architecture/test_observability_fitness.py -v

      - name: Resilience fitness functions
        run: pytest tests/architecture/test_resilience_fitness.py -v

      - name: Prompt governance fitness functions
        run: pytest tests/architecture/test_prompt_governance_fitness.py -v

      - name: Security fitness functions (Semgrep)
        run: semgrep --config .semgrep/fitness-functions.yml src/ --error
Fitness Function Maintenance

Fitness functions erode too if they are not maintained. The most common erosion is an expanding allow-list: developers add their module to ALLOWED_SDK_IMPORTERS to silence a failing test rather than fixing the import. Guard against this:
Review allow-list changes in PR review with the same scrutiny as production code changes.
The allow-list is itself a tracked artifact. Changes to it require the integration layer owner's approval in CODEOWNERS.
A fitness function whose allow-list grows by more than one entry per quarter is a signal that the architectural boundary it enforces is no longer holding and needs to be re-evaluated.
Fitness functions that have been silenced (by allow-lists or skip decorators) for more than one sprint without a tracked resolution must be flagged in the next architecture review.
Summary

Architectural fitness functions automate the enforcement of design decisions that would otherwise rely on human vigilance in code review. For Claude systems, the five categories — dependency boundaries, observability, security patterns, resilience wiring, and prompt governance — correspond to the most common sources of architectural erosion in mature Claude integrations. Running them in CI on every relevant change prevents the gradual drift that makes a clean architecture at v1.0 unrecognisable by v3.0.
Dependency fitness functions enforce that only the designated integration layer imports the Anthropic SDK and instantiates clients.
Observability fitness functions verify that every module making API calls also imports structlog and emits the required log fields.
Security fitness functions (Semgrep rules) detect user input in system prompts, raw message logging, and unguarded content block access.
Resilience fitness functions verify that circuit breakers are wired around API calls, not just defined.
Prompt governance fitness functions detect inline system prompt strings and enforce that all prompts are loaded from the template registry.
Allow-lists in fitness functions are themselves tracked artifacts; unreviewable allow-list growth signals architecture erosion.
